Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) said it was aware of the incident, which occurred during a static fire test at the launch pad.
“The FAA is aware that the Blue Origin New Glenn vehicle experienced an anomaly during a static fire test on the pad in Cape Canaveral, Florida around 9 p.m. local time on May 28,” the agency said in a statement.
Isaacman added that NASA would provide updates regarding any consequences for the Artemis programme and Moon Base initiatives.
NASA is aware of the anomaly that occurred tonight at Launch Complex 36 involving Blue Origin’s New Glenn rocket at Cape Canaveral Space Force Station. ⁰⁰Spaceflight is unforgiving, and developing new heavy-lift launch capability is extraordinarily difficult.
NASA chief Jared Isaacman said he and several senior engineers visited Blue Origin facilities and inspected damage at Launch Complex 36 following the incident.
Isaacman said NASA would continue working with the company as it investigates the anomaly and recovers from the setback, while reaffirming support for Blue Origin's lunar lander development programme and efforts to return New Glenn to flight.
Blue Origin chief executive Dave Limp said inspections had found several critical facilities and hardware at Launch Complex 36 remained intact despite the anomaly, although the site's support tower had been damaged and would require repairs."},{"url":"https://www.techtimes.com/articles/318300/20260612/blue-origin-targets-new-glenn-return-2026-launch-pad-recovery-real-test.htm","title":"Blue Origin Targets New Glenn Return in 2026: Launch Pad Recovery Is the Real Test","content":"The rocket and its only operational launch pad must recover after a May 28 static-fire explosion.

Their commitment follows a May 28 static-fire explosion that destroyed a New Glenn vehicle and damaged Launch Complex 36 at Cape Canaveral.
The May 28 accident occurred while New Glenn was secured at LC-36 for a static hot-fire test before its planned fourth flight.

On May 28, 2026, we experienced a significant anomaly during a New Glenn Integrated Launch Vehicle hotfire test at Launch Complex 36 in Florida. Now that we have more visibility into the impact, we know that we lost the lightning tower, the transporter-erector, and the hydraulic cylinders, but we caught a lot of breaks, too, and intend to make the most of them. We're going straight to a horizontal/vertical hybrid CONOPS we had already been developing for our 9x4 New Glenn launch vehicle, using existing infrastructure, skipping a new transporter-erector, and creating a common CONOPS across two pads. A crane performs a vertical breakover of New Glenn—the reverse of the operation already used to offload the booster from Jacklyn—and lifts the vehicle onto a refurbished launch table, where it mates to the launch vehicle hold-down ring. Damage at the site of a launchpad after an uncrewed Blue Origin New Glenn rocket exploded during a test at the Cape Canaveral Space Force Station in Florida Thursday.","score":0.921929,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_New_Glenn_rocket_explosion","title":"2026 New Glenn rocket explosion - Wikipedia","content":"Explosion of a New Glenn rocket. | NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman observes the damage to Launch Complex 36 from a helicopter following the New Glenn explosion |. On May 28, 2026, a New Glenn rocket operated by Blue Origin exploded during a static fire test at Cape Canaveral Space Force Station's Launch Complex 36 in Florida, U.S. The explosion destroyed the vehicle and severely damaged Blue Origin's only operational New Glenn launch site. Following the third flight of New Glenn on April 19, 2026, the Federal Aviation Administration opened an investigation after the rocket's second stage failed to reach its intended orbit. Blue Origin was preparing New Glenn's return to flight when the May 28 explosion occurred. Believed to be the most powerful rocket explosion since that of the Soviet N1 rocket#Second_failure,_serial_5L "N1 (rocket)") in 1969, the explosion destroyed the first stage along with the attached and fueled second stage, causing significant damage to Launch Complex 36, Blue Origin's only operational New Glenn launch site.","score":0.8745175,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1O90WZJALYc","title":"Blue Origin New Glenn rocket explodes during launch pad test at Cape Canaveral","content":"Blue Origin New Glenn rocket explodes during launch pad test at Cape Canaveral
Spaceflight Now
478000 subscribers
49213 likes
3965309 views
29 May 2026
A New Glenn rocket exploded at Cape Canaveral during a launch pad test firing on Thursday, May 28, 2026. The U.S. Space Force said there were no injuries at the Florida space port following the incident. 

The rocket was being prepared for a launch carrying a batch of satellites for Amazon's Leo internet constellation. The satellites were not on rocket for the launch pad static fire.

The explosion at Launch Complex 36 at Cape Canaveral Space Force Station caused extensive damage to the launch pad facility, toppling one of the 600-feet high lightning towers.

Video: Adam Bernstein and John Pisani, Spaceflight Now.

Learn more about the aftermath of the explosion: https://youtu.be/joUcagkuvQg?si=4YKHY7xZQWHBRPor

Blue Origin CEO Dave Limp said the company is "not rebuilding the same pad" where one of its New Glenn rockets recently exploded, in order to make way for a return to flight this year. In a note posted to Blue Origin's website, Limp said the company intends to adopt a "horizontal/vertical hybrid" launch configuration, using existing infrastructure that was already in development for its larger, more powerful New Glenn variant, called the "9x4.".","score":0.8047902,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":1.58,"request_id":"dbe49344-48e0-4405-93a9-e8ec3c290198"}
